Hi,

Thanks for a great library! I considered switching to parasail-python  (from SSW) in my projects due to the global alignment implementation. However, I observed a ~10-20x slowdown compared to ssw (using [this wrapper](https://github.com/vishnubob/ssw) of the ssw implementation). I was hoping that this speed difference happens in the conversion from python to C, and could easily be fixed. Below is the code I use. 

Any help regarding this is much appreciated!

```
import parasail
from time import time
import ssw  # using python wrapper from https://github.com/vishnubob/ssw

s1 = ""
s2 = ""

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.nw_trace_diag_16(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("parasail", total_elapsed)

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.ssw(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("parasail-ssw", total_elapsed)

start = time()
score_matrix = ssw.DNA_ScoreMatrix(match=2, mismatch=-1)
aligner = ssw.Aligner(gap_open=2, gap_extend=0, matrix=score_matrix)
result = aligner.align(s1, s2, revcomp=False)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("ssw", total_elapsed)
```

gives 
```
('parasail', 0.04381084442138672)
('parasail-ssw', 0.09930706024169922)
('ssw', 0.004579067230224609)
```

issue_title: Dropbox storage throws an exception when saving a file
issue_body:
Dropbox storage backend throws an exception when saving a file. I guess it stopped working after the API upgrade (see e46f3067fbc0ba9f8d1c351026a59aa9e0a390eb and #302).

How to reproduce:

    $ touch foo.txt
    $ echo "hello, world" >> foo.txt
    $ python3 manage.py shell
    >>> from storages.backends.dropbox import DropBoxStorage
    >>> dropbox_key = 'OAUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN'
    >>> dropbox_storage = DropBoxStorage(dropbox_key)
    >>> 
    >>> f = open('foo.txt')
    >>> dropbox_storage.save('foo.txt', f)

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.1/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/code.py", line 91, in runcode
    exec(code, self.locals)
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/files/storage.py", line 54, in save
    return self._save(name, content)
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/storages/backends/dropbox.py", line 111, in _save
    self.client.files_upload(content, self._full_path(name))
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dropbox/base.py", line 1267, in files_upload
    f,
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dropbox/dropbox.py", line 234, in request
    timeout=timeout)
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dropbox/dropbox.py", line 325, in request_json_string_with_retry
    timeout=timeout)
  File "/[...]/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dropbox/dropbox.py", line 369, in request_json_string
    type(request_binary))
TypeError: expected request_binary as binary type, got <class 'django.core.files.base.File'>
```

I've already found the bug: `DropBoxStorage._save` receives a `File` or any file-like object (as the `content` argument), and then passes it straight to the `self.client.files_upload` method. However, `DropboxBase.files_upload` accepts only `bytes`.

I'll provide a pull request fixing both this and #301, if you don't mind.

If I understand well, the `table_listeners` that put the tooling in place as well as the individual `audit_table` listeners that enable auditing for a single table are fired on an 'after_create' bound to a table instance. 

This seems to work only when directly using the mapper constructors like `create_all()`, because it uses the objects as defined, whereas using alembic migrations will lose track of the objects to which the listener is bound when creating the tables, preventing it to be ever fired. (see http://stackoverflow.com/a/16907683/1877609)

It would be amazing to have some kind of interface to either (i) easily generate migrations that would basically do the job of the listeners (ii) fire the listeners' content at will. 

Any thought on that?

issue_title: Support adding filter parameters to api calls
issue_body:
The TestRail API supports adding parameters to the endpoint URL to filter results returned. For instance, the get_runs endpoint (http://docs.gurock.com/testrail-api2/reference-runs#get_runs)  supports the following URL parameters:
```
:created_after	timestamp	Only return test runs created after this date (as UNIX timestamp).
:created_before	timestamp	Only return test runs created before this date (as UNIX timestamp).
:created_by	int (list)	A comma-separated list of creators (user IDs) to filter by.
:is_completed	bool	1 to return completed test runs only. 0 to return active test runs only.
:limit/:offset	int	Limit the result to :limit test runs. Use :offset to skip records.
:milestone_id	int (list)	A comma-separated list of milestone IDs to filter by.
:suite_id	int (list)	A comma-separated list of test suite IDs to filter by.
```

Add a filtering mechanism that allows the user to request filtered results from the API.

Question I have is how stable this API is as of date?

issue_title: New class needed for Tenant Object
issue_body:
There is a ticket field in jira: OPENSTACK-786, regarding “Tenant Name” support under Cloud->Tenants tab in NIOS GUI.

In order to populate this we need to call a new api:

https://10.39.12.179/wapidoc/objects/grid.cloudapi.tenant.html?highlight=tenant%20name#grid:cloudapi:tenant.name


It seems that the way to update the Tenant Name is to do a put to the Tenant object.

E.g.

curl -k -u cloud:infoblox -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X POST https://10.39.19.152//wapi/v2.2/record:host?_return_fields=ipv4addrs -d '{"ipv4addrs":[{"ipv4addr": "10.10.10.3"}],"name": "test","configure_for_dns": false,"extattrs": {"Tenant ID": {"value": "1013"},"VM ID": {"value": "355"},"Cloud API Owned": {"value": "True"},"VM Name": {"value": "SomeVM2"},"CMP Type":{"value":"OpenStack"},"Tenant Name": {"value": "SomeTenant"}}}'

then

curl -k -u cloud:infoblox -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X GET https://10.39.19.152/wapi/v2.2/grid:cloudapi:tenant?id=1013

to get the object handle

then

curl -k -u cloud:infoblox -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT https://10.39.19.152/wapi/v2.2/grid:cloudapi:tenant/ZG5zLnRlbmFudCQxMDEx:1013/1013 -d '{"name":"Some Tenant Name"}'

to update the Tenant Name on the Tenant Object.

Cloud API User needs to have RW permission on "All Tenant" permissions.

On each request list of _return_fields is sent to NIOS.
But if field that is present in _return_fields is not supported by current wapi_version then request fails.
For example EADefinition return_fields requires wapi_version 2.2, on wapi_version below 2.2 any request to this object will fail.
Currently possible workaround for package users are:
- set return_fields manually on search, where each listed field exists for selected wapi version;
- set retuen_fields=[], so default fields for defined on NIOS for particular wapi version to be returned;

Need to discuss possible ways to fix it (failing on lower wapi_version) on infoblox_client side.

issue_body:
Basically, I just updated from 1.2.x to 1.3.3, and the `version_table()` call is now broken. 

```
>>> version_table(common.database.RawWebPages)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/utils.py", line 136, in version_table
    if table.schema:
AttributeError: type object 'RawWebPages' has no attribute 'schema'

```

with the table defined as:

```


class RawWebPages(common.db_base.Base):
	__versioned__ = {}

	__tablename__     = 'raw_web_pages'
	name              = 'raw_web_pages'

	id                = Column(BigInteger, primary_key = True, index = True)
	state             = Column(common.db_types.dlstate_enum, default='new', index=True, nullable=False)
	errno             = Column(Integer, default='0')
	url               = Column(Text, nullable = False, index = True, unique = True)
	starturl          = Column(Text, nullable = False)
	netloc            = Column(Text, nullable = False, index = True)

	priority          = Column(Integer, default=1000000, index=True, nullable=False)
	distance          = Column(Integer, index=True, nullable=False)

	mimetype          = Column(Text)

	# Disabled due to disk-space issues.
	# raw_content       = Column(Text)

	filename     = Column(Text)

	fspath       = Column(Text)

	fetchtime         = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.min)
	addtime           = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow)

	ignoreuntiltime   = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.min, nullable=False)



```

The problem appears to be a result of https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-continuum/commit/7089b813c0b31de52c5b30d797a450d16e5f33e0.

I'm actually not sure where the "schema" attribute is supposed to come from, but probing the table class interactively yields:

```
[
	'__class__', 
	'__delattr__', 
	'__dict__', 
	'__dir__', 
	'__doc__', 
	'__eq__',

]
```

So it's not there. 

I'm not sure how this would ever work in the absense of the `.schema` member. I *assume* the code should actually be:

```
def version_table(table):
    """
    Return associated version table for given SQLAlchemy Table object.

    :param table: SQLAlchemy Table object
    """
    if hasattr(table, "schema"):
        return table.metadata.tables[
            table.schema + '.' + table.name + '_version'
        ]
    elif table.metadata.schema:
        return table.metadata.tables[
            table.metadata.schema + '.' + table.name + '_version'
        ]
    else:
        return table.metadata.tables[
            table.name + '_version'
        ]
```

Links to functions/classes/modules should be supported as much as possible. Together with "#1 Convert sphinx syntax to Markdown" the Sphinx references should be accepted and properly linked (ie. ``:class:`MyClass` ``.

However I want to propose an alternative similar syntax. Especially in Python, it is not necessary to annotate whether we're referring to a class, module or function, only the name is required. Some people, including myself, like adding the `()` for functions nevertheless, thus it should optionally be accepted. Example docstring with suggested format:

```python
def compress(segment, level):
  """
  Compress the #AudioSegment *segment* using the specified compression *level*
  and return a #bytes object. Decompress it with #decompress().
  """
```

issue_title: Image resize transforms causes negative values and values greater than 255
issue_body:
In my dataset, I performed two resize operations on the images. The first one is during data creation using cv2.resize and the second resize operation on the nd.array (using resize_short from mxnet.image or Resize from mxnet.gluon.data.vision.transforms). 

After the second resize operation, values in the image can be lower than 0 or greater than 255.

Attached is a python script to demonstrated the issue.

``` python code
import cv2
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet.image import resize_short
from mxnet.gluon.data.vision.transforms import Resize

img_in = "iam_image.png"
im = cv2.imread(img_in, cv2.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
im = np.asarray(im)

size1 = (1120, 800)

### First resize
image = cv2.resize(im, (size1[1], size1[0]))
print("image after cv2 resize: min {} max {}".format(np.min(image), np.max(image)))

image = mx.nd.array(image).expand_dims(axis=2)

### Second resize
#image = resize_short(image, int(800/3))
tf = Resize(size=(int(1120/3), int(800/2)))
image = tf(image)
print("image after mx resize min {} max {}".format(np.min(image), np.max(image)))

```
Test cases:
* Negative values and values greater than 255 only occur after both resize functions
* The errors exist for both Resize from gluon and resize_short on mxnet.image

* mxnet version = 1.2.0
* python version 3.6
* cv2 version 3.3.0
* on ubuntu 16.04.4

issue_title: Segmentation Fault when using large mx.nd.array
issue_body:
## Description
A segmentation fault can occur on some platforms when using large `mx.nd.array`s. The same code runs fine  using `numpy.array`.

This is the code causing the segfault:

```py
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
from random import random as r

# large array with 31x30x2 elements
# 30x30x2 does not cause a segfault
a = mx.nd.array([[[[[r()]] for a in range(31)] for b in range(30)] for c in range(2)])

# all three lines are important to reproduce the error
a = a.swapaxes(1, 2)
v1 = a[1:-1]
v2 = a[[0, -1]]

# creating a second array causes the segfault
mx.nd.array([])
```

And this is what happens when running the program:

issue_title: [MKLDNN Bug] MKLDNN eats lots of memory and then crash down.
issue_body:
MKLDNN eats lots of memory and crash down with **big** input image. You can try [a project of mine](https://github.com/chinakook/hr101_mxnet) to issue that. The ordinary MKL can run it successfully in 8GB memory, but MKLDNN eat all 64GB of my PC.
**Big** input data are very common used in little object detection in a big scene and regular scene segmentation.

issue_title: mx.io.ImageRecordIter confused me, does this function is related to time?
issue_body:
I use the latest mxnet code (0.11), and I want to study the usage of the mx.io.ImageRecordIter.
I use a simple image dataset which includes 16 images. This small dataset is downloaded from http://data.mxnet.io/data/test_images.tar.gz. I convert this image database as testim.rec

If I run the following code:

import mxnet as mx
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import time
import numpy as np

def get_iterators(datapath,batch_size,data_shape=(3,448,448)):
    dataiter = mx.io.ImageRecordIter(
        path_imgrec         = datapath,
        batch_size          = batch_size,
        data_shape          = data_shape,
        )
    return dataiter

batch_size=6
datapath='data/testim.rec'
dataiter = get_iterators(datapath, batch_size)
#time.sleep(2)

number=0
for batch in dataiter:
    data=batch.data[0]
    for i in range(batch_size):
        plt.subplot(3,batch_size, i+1+number*batch_size)

```{r mxnet iterator}

G_iterator<- function(batch_size){

    batch<- 0
    batch_per_epoch<-5

    reset<- function(){
        batch<<- 0
    }

    iter.next<- function(){
        batch<<- batch+1
        if (batch>batch_per_epoch) {
            return(FALSE)
        } else {
            return(TRUE)
        }
    }

    value<- function(){
        set.seed(123+batch)
        digit<- mx.nd.array(sample(0:9, size = batch_size, replace = T))
        data<- mx.nd.one.hot(indices = digit, depth = 10)
        data<- mx.nd.reshape(data = data, shape = c(1,1,-1, batch_size))
        return(list(data=data, digit=digit))
    }

    return(list(reset=reset, iter.next=iter.next, value=value, batch_size=batch_size, batch=batch))
}

D_iterator<- function(batch_size){

    batch<- 0
    batch_per_epoch<-5

    reset<- function(){
        batch<<- 0
    }

    iter.next<- function(){
        batch<<- batch+1
        if (batch>batch_per_epoch) {
            return(FALSE)
        } else {
            return(TRUE)
        }
    }

    value<- function(){
        set.seed(123+batch)
        idx<- sample(length(train_label), size = batch_size, replace = T)
        data<- train_data[,,,idx, drop=F]
        label<- mx.nd.array(train_label[idx])
        digit<- mx.nd.one.hot(indices = label, depth = 10)

        return(list(data=mx.nd.array(data), digit=digit, label=label))
    }

    return(list(reset=reset, iter.next=iter.next, value=value, batch_size=batch_size, batch=batch))
}

```

    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g1', kernel=c(4,4), num_filter=gen_features*4, no_bias=T) %>%

    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g2', kernel=c(3,3), stride=c(2,2), pad=c(1,1), num_filter=gen_features*2, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.BatchNorm(name='gbn2', fix_gamma=fix_gamma, eps=eps) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation(name='gact2', act_type='relu') %>%
    mx.symbol.Deconvolution(name='g3', kernel=c(4,4), stride=c(2,2),
                            pad=c(1,1), num_filter=gen_features, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.BatchNorm( name='gbn3', fix_gamma=fix_gamma, eps=eps) %>%

    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g4', kernel=c(4,4), stride=c(2,2), pad=c(1,1), num_filter=image_depth, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation( name='G_sym', act_type='tanh')

```{r Training Settings:device,loss and io}
devices<- mx.cpu()

data_shape_G<- c(1, 1, 10, batch_size)
data_shape_D<- c(28, 28, 1, batch_size)
digit_shape_D<- c(10, batch_size)

mx.metric.binacc <- mx.metric.custom("binacc", function(label, pred) {
  res <- mean(label==round(pred))
  return(res)
})

mx.metric.logloss <- mx.metric.custom("logloss", function(label, pred) {
  # KL Divergence D(x)log(D(x)) -D(x)log(D(G(z)))
  # 
  res <- mean(label*log(pred)+(1-label)*log(1-pred))
  return(res)
})

issue_title: Example of debugging gradient of op in HybridBlock
issue_body:
I have a custom LSTMCell and I get nan gradients after a certain number of gradient updates. I used pdb to step through the forward pass and check for nans. Tensorflow has an operation that adds nan checks to all ops in the graph, which would be nice here.

How can I debug to find out which op is causing the nans?

After googling, here is an approach. Is this the recommended approach? Or is there a simpler way?

1. Export the HybridBlock to json using: https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/gluon/gluon.html?highlight=export#mxnet.gluon.HybridBlock.export

2. Add a monitor to a symbol executor, which will print gradients of all ops: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/example/python-howto/monitor_weights.py

However, I'm not sure if hybird recurrent architectures can be exported.

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: test_loss.test_hinge_loss has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_title: user.get_roles does not include local roles inherited from groups when using "inherit=False"
issue_body:
As far as I know the `inherit=False` in `user.get_roles(user=foo, obj=bar, inherit=False)` is meant to ignore global roles. What it also does is ignore roles gotten from local roles of groups on the same object. That is inconsistent with `user.get_roles(user=foo, obj=bar, inherit=True)` which returns global and local roles owned by the user and local and gloabl roles inherited from groups the user is in.
The feature was added by @cillianderoiste in 654f749ff7a79eb7a21915b4a1b88f4870c51cd7.

I came across this creating a custom widget for selecting relations (for RelationChoice fields) but it seems to happen using the default widget too. The symptom is the same as this SO post:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27278300/relationlist-wrong-field-value-in-tile-schema

The objects stored in the tiles data are not RelationValues, but content objects, and become broken after a server restart.

That post gave some good hints, as far as I can tell the form handling for tiles is all done in plone.app.tiles rather than in z3c.form's applyForm(), so the correct IDataManager is never used.

Anyway, long story short, I've added this code to what seems like a logical place and it works for my widget and for the default one also. Branch is here:

https://github.com/hatchddigital/plone.app.tiles/commits/relationfield-handling

If this seems like a reasonable change let me know and I'll create a pull request, or if anyone has any hints or pointers as to a better fix I would appreciate that :).

issue_title: Compile Impala types when creating SQLAlchemy table
issue_body:
After some encouragement from @mrocklin at PyCon, gave Impyla SQLAlchemy a try.
It seems the Impala types may not be registered with the SQLAlchemy type compiler.

Simple demo:
```{python}
from impala.sqlalchemy import STRING, INT, FLOAT, TIMESTAMP
from sqlalchemy import Table, Column
from sqlalchemy.schema import MetaData

engine = sqlalchemy.engine.create_engine('impala://localhost')

metadata = MetaData(engine)

mytable = Table("mytable", metadata,
      Column('mytable_id', sqlalchemy.INT),
      Column('value', STRING)
)
mytable.create()

CompileError: (in table 'mytable', column 'value'): Compiler <sqlalchemy.sql.compiler.GenericTypeCompiler object at 0x1080d1810> can't render element of type <class 'impala.sqlalchemy.STRING'>
```

loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

async def main():
    context = zmq.asyncio.Context()
    socket = context.socket(zmq.DEALER)

issue_title: Ariba fails when using NFS directories for output
issue_body:
`shutil.rmtree` calls fail when the root_dir is an NFS volume. This due to how NFS handles files that are opened and then deleted while still open by silly renaming them.

Use `ariba run --noclean ...` or output to a non-NFS directory (and then move if needed) to circumvent issue.

Fix issue by explicitly closing files. I'll send through a pull request if I have time.

issue_title: How to communicate between apps pulsar, for example, through asyncio.Queue?
issue_body:
How to communicate between apps pulsar, for example, through asyncio.Queue?
The problem is that when the application starts somewhere inside the lost link to the asyncio.Queue object.
Prototype of uses:

```
import asyncio
import pulsar


class App1(pulsar.Application):
    queue = None

    async def watch_queue(self, worker):
        app = worker.app
        while 1:
            app.queue.put_nowait({'id': 1})  # Here app.queue is None

            await asyncio.sleep(1, loop=worker._loop)

    def worker_start(self, worker, exc=None):
        asyncio.ensure_future(self.watch_queue(worker), loop=worker._loop)


class App2(pulsar.Application):
    queue = None

    async def watch_queue(self, worker):
        app = worker.app
        app_queue = app.queue
        while 1:
            msg = app_queue.get()  # Here app.queue is None

            if not msg:
                await asyncio.sleep(1, loop=worker._loop)
                continue

            print(msg)

    def worker_start(self, worker, exc=None):
        asyncio.ensure_future(self.watch_queue(worker), loop=worker._loop)


class Server(pulsar.MultiApp):
    def build(self):
        App1.queue = asyncio.Queue()
        App2.queue = App1.queue

        yield self.new_app(App1)
        yield self.new_app(App2, prefix='app2')


async def run():
    server = Server()
    server.start()
```

issue_title: Incorrect UV corrdinates when loading simple .OBJ file. UV Array filled with last X vertex value.
Non json request parameters need to be coerced before being validated.
issue_body:
There are 2 issues here.

1) If there is a non json type request, all of the parameters are treated as strings (think of query string parameters or a form-encoded).  This ends up causing issues when their type attempts to get validated.  Some types (those that define a `__new__` coerce the type on creation.  Others that only override `__init__` (Object and Array) do not coerce the type.  

In the previous version of doctor [code in handle_http](https://github.com/upsight/doctor/blob/master/doctor/flask.py#L114-L115) would parse and coerce parameters as appropriate allowing the user to send a list type or dict type in query string params.  We will need to do something similar.

2) We discern if a parameter is not required if it has a default value in the function signature.  This works great, however if the default value is `None` the type will attempt to convert that value to it's internal native type.  This can cause a `TypeError` in the case of `Integer` or unexpected string values `'None'` instead of the value `None` for the `String` type.

Related to (2) are cases where passing None (null on the request) is expected to unset a value, vs not passing the param which would do nothing, vs passing a new value which sets that value.  We should consider how we might want to handle the below example:

```python
DEFAULT = object()

def update_foo(name: Name=DEFAULT):
    if name is not DEFAULT:
        # update foo's name
    elif name is None:
        # unset the name

    # if name is DEFAULT we don't want to change anything
```

issue_title: We need an event based risk calculator starting from the GMFs
issue_body:
In the past most (virtually all) risk calculations running in a cluster where dominated by the risk part, thus it made sense to recompute the GMFs on-the-fly every time. That had the advantage of reducing the data transfer significantly. For instance for Colombia the GMFs are 100 GB, the ruptures, 0.5 GB, so the data transfer is reduced by 200 times by computing the GMFs on the fly.

However things are different now. We introduced recently the `minimum_intensity` feature and that changes the rules of the game. It is now possible to filter away the GMFs below the minimum intensity, and that means that typically 99% of the GMFs can be removed (in the case of Colombia with minimum_intensity PGA=0.1 the 100 GB becomes 0.9 GB). This kind of reduction is typical for our calculations. So it makes sense to compute the GMFs once and to store them since after the filtering they are few and easy to transfer.

Also, now the GMFs are stored in the HDF5 format in an ultra-efficient way (and they will be stored in an even more efficient way in the future, using a new feature of h5py ) so it is not an issue to store even terabytes of them. A storing speed of 500+ MB/s has been measured on Wilson. Transferring hundreds of the gigabytes is achievable too, if the transfer is done carefully, i.e. if you don't try to transfer everything at once.

Due to those advances, saving the GMFs has become reasonable now. Having the GMFs on disk allow different parallelization strategies for the risk that will result in a big speedup. Moreover computing the GMFs in the risk part has a big memory cost, whereas computing them in the hazard part has a much smaller memory cost due to the differences in the algorithm used.

Having a GMF-based calculator may also be a necessary preliminary step for the UCERF calculation, since it is too large to be done with the current calculator (it will likely run out of memory).

Another important use case is the case of a workstation/laptop: when there are few cores, the hazard part is typically dominating the risk calculation, so it makes sense to cache the GMFs to support non-cluster situations.

NB: the current calculator based on the rupture has to be maintained because there are still computations dominated by the risk part and where it makes sense to recompute the GMFs on the fly.
The user will be able to select the old calculator or the new one by specifying the calculation_mode in the job.ini file. `event_based_risk` or `ebr_from_gmf`.

PS: having a calculator starting from the GMFs is needed for two other reasons, apart from performance:
1. it makes it easier to debug and test the calculator
2. a third party can give us the GMFs and we can start the risk from there.

issue_body: The Response Syntax and Response Structure for the paginator list_objects_v2 (https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/develop/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Paginator.ListObjectsV2) has the NextToken as part of the response, however list_objects_v2 actually returns NextContinuationToken.  The list_objects_v2 client properly has this defined, but not the  paginator.

While I do understand the desire to provide methods very similar (if not analogous) to the AWS CLI, I really feel like working with boto3 does not really improve on the AWS CLI. From my point of view, boto3 should not only be a wrapper, but also an adapter to make the CLI something desirable to work with from python. For instance, instead of creating an S3 client with ``boto3.client('s3')``, hide this and use a ``S3Client`` class. Instead of managing multi-part upload with the upload id directly, it would be more 'pythonic' to have a ``MultiPartUpload`` class, and have the corresponding methods take or return an instance of this class. What is the point of having the ``Client`` do operations such as get_bucket_policy(Bucket='string')? Wouldn't it be much better (and intuitive) to have a getter for bucket_policy on the Bucket class? Also, having helper methods would help a lot. Something like ``Bucket.upload_file(file, path)``, or along those lines. The point isn't precisely the examples I have pointed out here, the point is the library is hard to work with, it is essentially the same as the CLI. Please note I do not mean any disrespect and this is just, at least from my point of view, constructive criticism.

issue_title: Ability to set ResponseMetadata in stubber's add_client_error method
Waiters can be bad friends. How about retriers?
issue_body:
I'm seeing the following awkward behavior, but it's an instance of a broader issue with waiters in general (using boto3 here, but it's just calling through to botocore for the actual logic):

```python
# Context is setting up a cross-account peering connection
conn = boto3.resource('ec2').VpcPeeringConnection(pcx_id)
conn.wait_until_exists()
conn.accept()
```

This will occasionally throw a `ClientError` with `InvalidVpcPeeringConnectionID.NotFound` on the `conn.accept()` call.

The issue, as I understand it, is that `wait_until_exists()` calls `DescribeVpcPeeringConnections` repeatedly until it gets a result. Unfortunately, there's no guarantee that the metadata infrastructure that responds to my `DescribeVpcPeeringConnections` call will be the same one that responds to my `AcceptVpcPeeringConnection` call, and if you're particularly unlucky (as I appear to have been recently) the former will see your peering connection when it hasn't propagated to the latter yet.

Given how AWS APIs are structured, I don't think there's much we can do better if we stick to the same user-facing API, but ultimately all I want is for my `.accept()` call to succeed, and the `.wait_until_exists()` call was an implementation detail for that. So how about adding a botocore concept of an "eventual consistency retrier" (better name proposals welcome!) that uses much of the same information to let me retry operations like `AcceptVpcPeeringConnection` automatically. For example, [this code](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/ec2/2016-11-15/waiters-2.json#L568-L570) is basically enough information to know to retry `AcceptVpcPeeringConnection` automatically and to ignore (possibly with exponential backoff or similar) `InvalidVpcPeeringConnectionID.NotFound` errors.

I'm not too concerned about how the notion is presented to the user (we'd want to be able to opt in because if you aren't expecting a resource to show up, there's not much point in waiting) but it seems like this could be a useful building block.

Any thoughts? Is there a better way to get reliable "wait until X exists then do something with it" behavior with today's machinery?

issue_title: ciso8601 should specify and enforce a code style
issue_body:
The codebase of ciso8601 is a mix of various C coding/formatting styles.

The classic example in C is where opening `{` should go. In ciso8601, we have examples of both:

* [Same line](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601/blob/e886109dc8f6a7168a1d4d38ca3b48ef26a03610/module.c#L187)
* [Next line](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601/blob/e886109dc8f6a7168a1d4d38ca3b48ef26a03610/module.c#L145)

`ciso8601` should specify a C formatting style and enforce it. 
This could then be put into a [`.clang-format` file](https://clang.llvm.org/docs/ClangFormatStyleOptions.html), so that developer's IDE's could enforce the style as they code.

While I don't care which format is chosen, I do want ciso8601 to have a consistent style and for my contributions to match that style.

ISO 8601 allows for a special case of midnight: `24:00:00`.

For example `2007-04-05T24:00` is the same instant as `2007-04-06T00:00`

But ciso8601 currently supports the latter:

```
In [1]: import ciso8601

In [2]: ciso8601.parse_datetime('2007-04-06T00:00')
Out[2]: datetime.datetime(2007, 4, 6, 0, 0)

In [3]: ciso8601.parse_datetime('2007-04-05T24:00')
Out[3]: None
```

Tested on ciso8601 version 1.0.7

issue_title: CallTraceStoreLogger should store only unique traces by default
issue_body:
Storing only unique traces in the logger has a couple advantages:
1) Reduces memory overhead of tracing.
2) Should improve annotation quality by improving diversity of queried traces.

There doesn't seem to be much use case for seeing the actual distribution of traces, but if someone needs that, it's only a few lines of code to implement their own logger.

For example, consider this network object:

```http
{
    "parent_id": null,
    "state": "allocated",
    "prefix_length": 16,

issue_title: Add a --first-feature option to the geocoder
issue_body:
Instead of getting a featurecollection with the first 5 results, many use cases at the command line will require selecting a single result, usually the first feature.

I've been using this pattern to get a GeoJSON feature
```
mapbox geocoding "place" | jq -c .features[0]
```

Useful for batch geocoding
```
cat places.txt | parallel mapbox geocoding {} | jq -c .features[0] | fio collect
```
 
But `jq` isn't installed everywhere (it *should* be, it's awesome) and the arguments are not immediately clear.

**Proposal**

A `--first-feature` option to output the first, highest-ranked Feature (without line-breaks to facilitate streaming) rather than a FeatureCollection.

The parallel batch geocoding becomes a little cleaner:

```
cat places.txt | parallel mapbox geocoding --first-feature | fio collect
```

The only reason I'd hesitate to implement this would be our design principles #10:
> We'll rely on other programs like sed, awk, jq for finer parsing of responses from APIs at first, absorbing some of this when it makes sense.

So, does `--first-feature` make sense? Or do we just rely on `jq`?

issue_title: pipenv install --keep-outdated updates more than I expect
issue_body:
##### Expected result
I ran `pipenv install nose --dev --keep-outdated`, expecting to install `nose` but nothing else.

##### Actual result
`python-dateutil` was also updated, and `pycodestyle`'s hashes changed.

I'm especially confused as to why those two: `pipenv update --dry-run` tells me I have several other packages that need updates too, and `pipenv graph` tells me `nose` has no dependencies (and I think it's right).

Note that we have a VCS dependency, but it has no dependencies itself, so I think this is unrelated to the other issues with `--keep-outdated` and VCS deps.

Here are the changes it made:

issue_title: pipenv doesn't understand some version markers
issue_body:
Pipenv fails to parse some python version markers
given this Pipfile:
```

[[source]]
name = "pypi"
verify_ssl = true
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"

[packages]

[dev-packages]
coverage = "*"

[requires]
python_version = "3.5"
```

when running pipenv internally runs resolver.py it fails because it is unable to properly parse the version markers of the coverage packages.
so given that Pipfile if I run `pipenv lock` it crashes (exit code 1) because  resolver.py fails

issue_title: tensorflow/html5lib "Your dependencies could not be resolved" but can't see problem
issue_body:
```
$ pipenv install --dev

        - six [required: Any, installed: 1.11.0]

    - html5lib [required: ==0.9999999, installed: 0.9999999]
      - six [required: Any, installed: 1.11.0]
    - markdown [required: >=2.6.8, installed: 2.6.11]

  - wheel [required: >=0.26, installed: 0.30.0]
```

So we have:
```
html5lib [required: >=0.999,<0.99999999,!=0.9999,!=0.99999, installed: 0.9999999]
```
and
```
html5lib [required: ==0.9999999, installed: 0.9999999]
```

That package uses a pretty horrible versioning scheme, but the versions don't seem to conflict.

One requires `0.9999999` (seven zeros, what we have installed) and the other requires `<0.99999999` (eight zeros). Should be ok?

issue_title: Consider diff management options for patched vendored projects
issue_body:
One of the challenges with carrying patches indefinitely for vendored projects is that it can easily lead to those projects becoming stale, and potentially subject to security vulnerabilities already fixed in the upstream project.

#1887 covers switching the regular vendored dependencies over to a redistributor-friendly model akin to the one that `pip` already uses, but that won't apply directly to the patched dependencies in `pipenv` (including `pip` 9.0.x itself).

One option worth considering is `git subtree`, since that takes full advantage of git metadata to help with pulling in upstream changes while retaining the downstream modifications. (I use it for this purpose at work, although I haven't looked at the specifics of how we might apply it to `pipenv`)

issue_title: SimpleConsumer.stop() ought to commit offsets if appropriate
issue_body:
I'm spinning this out of #206 which we won't be merging.

Currently, if we call `stop()` on a consumer, the auto-commit thread will abort, possibly without committing the latest partition offsets. Instead, `stop()` should make sure the latest offsets are committed, provided that 1) `self.auto_commit_enable == True` and 2) we didn't call `stop()` as part of some exception handling (this second rule is up for discussion, I suppose).

We might be able to cherry-pick some commits out of #206 towards this.

issue_title: Implement function to get interpolation weights
issue_body:
We should implement a function equivalent to ``get_interp_weights`` from healpy

cc @woodmd

Sphinx provides a mechanism, which is not normally supported in Python, to provide docstrings for class attributes and module-level variables.  We use this in several places in Astropy to document such objects.  The syntax places a docstring immediately after the variable/attribute is defined like:

```
answer = 42
"""Answer to the question of life, the universe, and everything."""
```

Sphinx can pick these up and add docs for the `answer` variable to the documentation for whatever module or class it's defined in.

Astropy has a metaclass called `InheritDocstrings` which allows subclasses to automatically inherit docstrings from their base class for any members they override (and for which they do not explicitly provide docstrings).  However, this mechanism doesn't work for class attributes since there isn't any officially supported way to handle them in Python.  And would be nice to write a Sphinx extension and/or patch to Sphinx to make this work properly.

I tried to create the documentation for a project with python 3.5 and received some warnings.

Sphinx wasn't able to resolve `collections.Iterable`, `collections.Generator`, `collections.Iterator`, `collections.Callable` and `collections.MutableMapping`. This is almost the exact problem I had in #237 but with other types.

issue_title: Terminate Function Seems Not Working Properly With SLURM
issue_body:
This issue only matters when using Vasp Monitors and only when running jobs in a SLURM environmental. It seems to originate from the Popen.terminate() python API. If a job terminates normally, all the function works properly. However, once a VASP job is killed by Popen.terminate(), which would be true if the Monitor finds an error, new VASP job will never be able to run. The error message is "Unable to create job step: Job/step already completing or completed". The power of custodian is **fully prohibited** in this situation.

How do you know whether you are affected? If the success rate of recent calculation degrades, probably you are already affected.

I reproduced the error using a simple python script without custodian and asked NERSC staff to fix it. They confirmed the issue and promised to investigate it. However, it has already been two weeks I didn't get any update. As a precaution, I think we should consider the possibility they are not able fix it in short term.

I have an alternative to walk-around this issue in my fork. The point is to use "scancel" to kill VASP job. This method releases the resource successfully while Popen.terminate() doesn't. It seems to be working fine for the last two weeks. However, it makes the custodian code more complicated and environment dependent. I am not sure whether it is a good idea to merge this piece of code to the main repository.

issue_title: bug in multichannel non-local means if channels != 3
issue_body:
## Description
The non-local means Cython code currently assumes 3 channels when computing multi-channel patch weights, but there is no check that the input images are actually 3 channel.

code that assumes three channels: [here](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/cf3cdaf35797b0ebbaf155c91ecf5f8b761e0ea5/skimage/restoration/_nl_means_denoising.pyx#L108) and [here](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/cf3cdaf35797b0ebbaf155c91ecf5f8b761e0ea5/skimage/restoration/_nl_means_denoising.pyx#L467-L472)
Other code outside of the weights computation [already supports arbitrary number of channels](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/cf3cdaf35797b0ebbaf155c91ecf5f8b761e0ea5/skimage/restoration/_nl_means_denoising.pyx#L637)

If the input images have more than 3 channels, denoising will still occur, but the patch weights will be determined based on only the first 3 channels.  In the off chance that a two-channel image is used, out-of-bounds memory access can occur during computation of the patch weights.

The Cython code should be updated to fully support an arbitrary number of channels.  One real world use case for > 3 channels:  [hyperspectral imaging](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperspectral_imaging).  I volunteer to make a PR to do this.

## Way to reproduce
see the example code linked that demonstrates where the problem lies

alternatively, modify the top of the non-local means Cython code to set `boundscheck=True` and then try denoising only the first two channels of an RGB image.  You will see a lot of messages regarding out-of-bounds access on the memory-views.

issue_title: The doc says it returns an image, but the modification is actually in-place
issue_labels: [{'id': 233604065, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/labels/difficulty:%20novice', 'name': 'difficulty: novice', 'color': 'e8c4dc', 'default': False}, {'id': 298491430, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'ea4041', 'default': False}, {'id': 298491501, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/labels/type:%20documentation', 'name': 'type: documentation', 'color': 'fbda04', 'default': False}]
issue_body: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/93e9d90bb3bb9d04e55a5087996e87efe2fb8fdb/skimage/draw/draw.py#L312

Hi,

There is a bug there:

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/feature/peak.py#L152

this line assumes that we are working with 2D arrays while the rest of the code is for ND arrays.

I propose the following fix:

intensities = image.flat[np.ravel_multi_index(coordinates,image.shape)]

I usually follow the rule "if it's not broken, don't fix it", so you can ignore the following suggestion:

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/feature/peak.py#L149

coordinates = np.transpose((image > peak_threshold).nonzero())

could be written:

coordinates = np.argwhere(image > peak_threshold)

See http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.argwhere.html

"np.argwhere(a) is the same as np.transpose(np.nonzero(a))."

Kind regards,

Kevin

issue_title: setup.py not properly distinguishing source, build, and install locations
issue_body:
There are a bunch of odd things with the build that need to be fixed.

- The `.i` and generated `.cxx` files get installed in regular non-develop builds which should be avoided
- The <repo>/build directory is created (even when a different install location is specified) and some build artifacts are copied there. But it doesn't seem to serve any purpose. We should remove it as we don't want any arbitrary hard-coded locations in our setup.py file. And it shouldn't be necessary since setuptools should automatically create a temp dir for intermediate steps
- The `.capnp` files are not included in the specified installation location. They are getting moved to the hard-coded `build/...` location
- Build artifacts like the `.so` files are copied into the source tree as well as the installation location. They should only be copied to the installation location (or the repo location if it is a developer build?)
- In develop mode, a nupic.egg-link file specifies the repo location. In this case, all generated files or files from nupic.core must be copied to the repo location or additionally need to be included in the `.egg-link` file.

Relates to #1670 
CC @rhyolight @oxtopus @david-ragazzi

issue_title: OS X binaries don't work with Homebrew Python [OS X 10]
issue_body:
## Environment
- OS X 10.10
- Python: Homebrew Python 2.7.9

## To Reproduce

1. `pip uninstall nupic` to ensure no existing installation
1. `pip install nupic` (installs one of the binaries below)
1. `python -c "import nupic.bindings.math"`

### Error:

```
Fatal Python error: PyThreadState_Get: no current thread
[1]    77717 abort      python -c "import nupic.bindings.math"
```

I tried this manually by downloading both the wheels below and got the same result with either one:

- [nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_9_intel.whl](https://pypi.python.org/packages/cp27/n/nupic/nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_9_intel.whl#md5=d7843d31e9c6b96c8fc727e6ea0e672c)
- [nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl](https://pypi.python.org/packages/2.7/n/nupic/nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl#md5=3d9d57cd0a8ee9c795209a8b554b683a)

To install from one of these manually, just download them and `pip install path/to/wheel`.

## Potential Cause

Unpacking and inspecting the wheel file gives us a look into how the `_math.so` binary was built on Travis-CI:

```
∙ cp nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.whl nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.zip
∙ unzip nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel.zip
∙ cd nupic-0.1.2-cp27-none-macosx_10_10_intel
∙ find . -name _math.so
./nupic/bindings/_math.so
∙ otool -L nupic/bindings/_math.so
nupic/bindings/_math.so:
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1213.0.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.6)
	/usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 120.0.0)
```

So `_math.so` was built on Travis-CI using the local system python as a shared library. Because `/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python` exists on my local system as the default Python implementation, there is a mismatch in python versions when using this file.

As a comparison, I wanted to `otool` the `_math.so` file that gets generated locally when I build NuPIC from source, so after running a build locally using homebrew python I ran `otool` on the locally generated `_math.so` file:

```
∙ otool -L nupic/bindings/_math.so
nupic/bindings/_math.so:
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1213.0.0)
	/usr/local/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libkj-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libcapnp-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/local/lib/libcapnpc-0.6-dev.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 120.0.0)
```

This works fine with homebrew Python because `/usr/local/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python` soft links to `/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python`.

issue_title: We should have a way of using the SparseMatrix with segments
issue_body:
We don't currently have a way to write fast Python algorithms if the algorithms require variable number of segments per cell. Writing fast Python algorithms requires batch operations, i.e. no looping in Python. Our Connections class supports segments but it doesn't support batch operations. Our SparseMatrix supports batch operations, but we don't have a good way of using it with segments, at least not with Python code.

Possible solutions:

1. Add batch operations to Connections
2. Make it so that our Python code can use the SparseMatrix for algorithms that require segments

This issue tracks Solution 2.

issue_title: nupic.bindings must support standalone source builds
issue_body:
The nupic.bindings build currently relies on some previous steps to build the C++ static libraries. We need to add these steps and the appropriate files to the source build of nupic.bindings to enable source builds from the egg or via PyPI.

Tasks:

issue_title: Refactor PyRegion subclasses to take specific proto object in read
issue_body: Instead of taking a `RegionImpl` proto object, they should implement `getProtoType` and the `PyRegion` base class should call `getProtoType` and call `read` on the subclass with the specific proto object returned by `getProtoType`.
Currently, the version is specified in both places. The Doxygen file should reuse the VERSION file.

issue_title: Adding synapses over the limit causes exception to be thrown
issue_body: `Connections.cpp` defines a fixed limit for number of synapses per segment, `maxSegmentsPerCell_`. Currently, when the user tries to add a synapse over the limit, the method throws and exception. This is undesirable in production code since it means that some datasets will cause the code to fail. One workaround is to set `maxSegmentsPerCell_` very high, but it would be desirable to maintain fixed resource consumption by recycling old synapses with low permanence. This approach is currently taken in the deprecated `Cells4` C++ implementation.

issue_title: Include data from authority records while indexing
issue_body:
Originally by *cdickins* on 2011-07-06

* when indexing fields that are under authority control, get the relevant data from referenced authority records and include it in the word, pair and phrase index tables

- if an authority record is added or modified, make sure the dependent fields (and ONLY the dependent fields) of other (typically bibliographic) records are reindexed upon request, even though their 'modification_date' timestamp may not have changed. E.g.: re-indexing all recently modified records for field '100!__a' should not only re-index field '100!__a' for recently modified authority records, but should also re-index field '100!__a' of bibliographic records that have NOT been recently updated, but that are under authority control and in field '100!__0' refer to a recently updated authority record and hence need to be reindexed, including the data e.g. of the '100!__a', '400!__a' and '500!__a' fields of the recently updated authority record.

issue_labels: [{'id': 414883473, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/labels/Lane:%20fast', 'name': 'Lane: fast', 'color': 'ffa868', 'default': False}, {'id': 411997049, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/labels/Status:%20in%20work', 'name': 'Status: in work', 'color': '38a828', 'default': False}, {'id': 209124211, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/labels/Type:%20bug', 'name': 'Type: bug', 'color': 'ff2a00', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
**Problem:**
We are encountering this issue: inveniosoftware/flask-security-fork#38 when building with devel dependencies.
As it will be fixed in flask-security we should remove flask-security-fork from devel for now and add flask-security once we switch to it.

The package contains `examples/app.py` example application, but there is no test
for it in `tests/test_example_app.py`.  It should be added.  See existing examples:

- https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records/blob/master/tests/test_example_app.py
- https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/blob/master/tests/test_example_app.py

Dear all, 

Currently the default sorter factory is filtering the options given by configuration to keep only the ordering. 

https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/19fdd5b38d00f37d8968f5d887b0fcfb6d233254/invenio_records_rest/sorter.py#L149

In some case it is possible to ask for sorting on a field that is sometimes missing from some record thanks to the "missing" parameter: 

https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/search-request-sort.html#_missing_values

It is at my knowledge not possible to do so in Invenio. 

What I would like to do if it is approved. Is to alter the code of `sorter.py` to keep the missing option. 

To do so I see several solution, we can turn fields in a dictionary and put the options that should reach elastic search in this dict. 

```
RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS` = dict(
    records=dict(
        bestmatch=dict(
            title=_('Relevance'),
            fields=dict(name='control_number', missing='_last'),
            default_order='desc',
            order=1,
        ),
    ),
)
```


We can also prefix the options name in the RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS dict. 

```
RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS` = dict(
    records=dict(
        bestmatch=dict(
            title=_('Relevance'),
            fields=['control_number'],
            default_order='desc',
            order=1,
            ES_missing='_last',
        ),
    ),
)
```

I am open to all suggestions and really happy to get feedbacks.

Bests,

issue_title: C BTrees allow some unorderable types
issue_body:
Given this class:
```python
class Bad(object):
    def __eq__(self, other):
        return False
```

Under Python 2.7, you can use instances of this class as a key in any size C BTree. 

Under Python 3, you can insert this into an empty tree only. Once you do, the tree is broken.  Jim Fulton suggested that the key should be compared to itself on an empty tree to catch this (but given that pointer equality is checked first, it's not clear exactly how that would be done).

issue_title: ffpyplayer can't be built with new toolchain
issue_body:
`ffpyplayer` and it's dependency `ffmpeg` have no recipe for new toolchain (while both of them work in old toolchain). Current `ffmpeg` recipe [is broken and has nothing to do](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/issues/920) with one ffpyplayer needs.

New toolchain can be used with `sdl2` recipe only, that's mean we should use `ffpyplayer==4.0.0` and `sdl2` as dependency for it (while old toolchain uses `ffpyplayer==3.2` and `sdl`). I tried to create recipe for v4.0.0, but got no success.

Since `ffpyplayer` is probably [only way](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/kivy-users/ypJOZXlUEqM) to support video on Android devices it makes impossible to use video in Kivy on Android using new toolchain.

Btw, there's no docs about video in Kivy on Android it makes almost impossible for new users to understand what happening.

issue_title: Orientation change causes bogus SDL_QUIT and SDL_APP_TERMINATING events
issue_body:
I am using PySDL2 + python-for-android *without kivy*. In my test app, the first orientation change seems to cause an `SDL_QUIT` and an `SDL_APP_TERMINATING` event, and I don't know why - it looks like a bug to me.

This is the test code (feel free to build & test for yourself, needs `--requirements=sdl2,pysdl2,python3crystax`, `--orientation user` of course, and possibly `--window` to reproduce):
```python3
#!/usr/bin/env python3

def apply_hack():
    import ctypes.util
    orig_func = ctypes.util.find_library
    def android_find_library_hack(*args):
        import os
        name = args[0]

        # Truncate ending for easier comparison:
        if name.find(".so.") >= 0:
            name = name.partition(".so.")[0]
        if name.endswith(".so"):
            name = name[:-len(".so")]
        if not name.endswith("."):
            name += "."

        # Helper function to check lib name:
        def check_name(lib_name, search_name):
            if filename.endswith('.so') and (
                    filename.startswith("lib" + search_name) or
                    filename.startswith(search_name)):
                return True
            if search_name.endswith("-2.0."):  # PySDL2 hack
                search_name = search_name[:-len("-2.0.")] + "."
                return check_name(lib_name, search_name)
            return False

        # Check the user app lib dir and system dir:
        app_root = os.path.normpath(os.path.abspath(os.path.join(
            os.path.dirname(__file__), '..', '..', 'lib')))
        sys_dir = "/system/lib"
        for lib_search in [app_root, sys_dir]:
            if not os.path.exists(lib_search):
                continue
            for filename in os.listdir(lib_search):
                if check_name(filename, name):
                    return os.path.join(lib_search, filename)
        return orig_func(*args)
    import ctypes.util
    ctypes.util.find_library = android_find_library_hack
apply_hack()

import ctypes
import sdl2 as sdl
import sys
import time
import traceback

window = None
renderer = None

def test_app():
    global window, renderer
    print("Creating window (app launch)...")
    sdl.SDL_Init(sdl.SDL_INIT_EVERYTHING)
    window = sdl.SDL_CreateWindow(
        "This is a test.".encode("utf-8", "replace"),
        sdl.SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED, sdl.SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED,
        640, 480, sdl.SDL_WINDOW_SHOWN |
        sdl.SDL_WINDOW_RESIZABLE)
    renderer = sdl.SDL_CreateRenderer(window, -1, 0)

    # Main event loop:
    while True:
        time.sleep(0.01)

        # Fetch events:
        events = []
        while True:
            ev = sdl.SDL_Event()
            result = sdl.SDL_PollEvent(ctypes.byref(ev))
            if result == 1:
                events.append(ev)
                continue
            break
        for event in events:
            handle_event(event)

def handle_event(event):
    global window, renderer

    if event.type == sdl.SDL_QUIT or \
            event.type == sdl.SDL_APP_TERMINATING:
        event_name = "SDL_QUIT"
        if event.type == sdl.SDL_APP_TERMINATING:
            event_name = "SDL_APP_TERMINATING"
        print("Terminating (app termination event: " +
            event_name + ").")
        print("Actually, ignoring this")
        #if renderer != None:
        #    sdl.SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer)
        #    renderer = None
        #if window != None:
        #    sdl.SDL_DestroyWindow(window)
        #    window = None
        #sys.exit(0)
    elif event.type == sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT:
        if event.window.event == \
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_CLOSE:
            # Throw away renderer:
            if renderer != None:
                sdl.SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer)
                renderer = None
                print("Renderer destroyed (window closed).")
            # Close window:
            if window != None:
                sdl.SDL_DestroyWindow(window)
                window = None
                print("Window destroyed (window closed).")
                # Terminate if not on android:
                if sdl.SDL_GetPlatform().decode("utf-8").\
                        lower() != "android":
                    print("Terminating app since on desktop OS, " +
                        "and last window was closed.")
                    sys.exit(0)
        if (event.window.event ==
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_RESTORED or
                event.window.event ==
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_EXPOSED or
                event.window.event ==
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_MAXIMIZED):
            # Re-create renderer if necessary:
            if renderer == None:
                print("Renderer re-created (got window focus).")
                renderer = sdl.SDL_CreateRenderer(window, -1, 0)
    elif (event.type == sdl.SDL_APP_DIDENTERBACKGROUND):
        print("Entered background.")
        # Wipe renderer:
        if renderer != None:
            sdl.SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer)
            renderer = None
            print("Renderer destroyed (entered background).")
    elif (event.type == sdl.SDL_APP_WILLENTERFOREGROUND):
        print("Entering foreground.")
        # Re-create window if necessary:
        if window == None:
            window = sdl.SDL_CreateWindow(
                "This is a test.".encode("utf-8", "replace"),
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED, sdl.SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED,
                640, 480, sdl.SDL_WINDOW_SHOWN |
                sdl.SDL_WINDOW_RESIZABLE)
            if renderer != None:
                sdl.SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer)
                renderer = None
                print("Renderer destroyed (new window).")
        # Re-create renderer if necessary:
        if renderer == None:
            renderer = sdl.SDL_CreateRenderer(window, -1, 0)
            print("Renderer re-created (entering foreground).")

test_app()
```

issue_title: Standardize transform should handle edge case where variable has zero variance
issue_body:
In edge cases where a variable has zero variance, standardize should do the same as center rather than producing an error.

Suppose I am fitting a linear model with a large number of random covariates.  I'd like to standardize them and fit a regularized model (e.g. sklearn.linear_model.Ridge).  If there's some probability that a given covariate has zero variance, I'd like my code to run (and give that covariate a coefficient of zero) rather than crash.

Toy example:

```
import patsy
data = {"x1": [1, 2, 3, 4], "x2":[5, 5, 5, 5]}
mat = patsy.dmatrix("standardize(x1) + center(x2)", data)  # Works
mat = patsy.dmatrix("standardize(x1) + standardize(x2)", data)  # Fails! Should handle this edge case
```

issue_title: DataReader should raise an exception when a data_source is not implemented
issue_body:
Hello,

I think it will be more explicit if DataReader raised an exception when data_source is not
in  `("yahoo", "yahoo-actions", "google", "fred", or "ff")``

Maybe something like

    if data_source == "yahoo":
        return ...
    ...
    elif data_source == "famafrench":
        return ...
    else:
        raise(NotImplementedError("data_source=%r is not implemented" % data_source))

Kind regards

One of the nice things about data from FRED is the accompanying metadata: frequency, units, seasonal adjustment... `fredapi` has a nice [implementation](https://github.com/mortada/fredapi/blob/master/fredapi/fred.py#L80) for fetching that.

Is there an interest in providing that in pandas_datareader?  If so, I'll volunteer to implement it.  The main decisions would be a) do we return metadata as a separate object or pin it into `df._metadata`?  and b) do we want to wrap `fredapi` in some way or do it from scratch?

The class `SPARQLStore` calls this `init` from the `NSSPARQLWrapper` superclass

`super(SPARQLStore, self).__init__(endpoint, returnFormat=XML, **sparqlwrapper_kwargs)`

the problem is that this doesn't allow any other return format other than XML (for example, JSON). The reason is:

- `returnFormat=XML` sets the default returnFormat upstream to XML
- the `query()` functions resets the query setup at every query, using `self.resetQuery()`

so, even if I call `setReturnFormat()` from the base class `SPARQLWrapper`, it has no effect. Results always return as XML. By removing `returnFormat=XML`, I should be allowed to call `SPARQLStore` like this

`SPARQLStore (endpoint='...', returnFormat=JSON`

This proposal suggests switching from the current "NFF" binary format for saving datatable Frames on disk, to a new format codenamed `Jay`. The new format saves the data into a single file (not folder) and keeps meta information in `Cap'n Proto` format (not CSV).

It is believed that the new format will be an improvement over NFF in several respects:
1. Single file is easier to pass around from one machine to another.
2. For a dataset containing multiple columns (10s or 100s of thousands), the operating system becomes very sluggish with any file-related operation.
3. Frames containing many columns but few rows waste a lot of disk space in NFF format due to each file having a minimum size of 4096 bytes.
4. A frame containing `n` columns requires between `n` and `2n` open file handles or memmapped regions, which becomes difficult on systems where those resources are limited. This becomes especially problematic in row-by-row operations on the frame.
5. The architecture that forces us to keep many memmap regions opened at the same time is prone to sporadic memory errors.
6. Cap'n Proto storage of meta information is more flexible and can be parsed faster than CSV.

The NFF format has the following advantages over Jay:
1. Multi-file storage is advantageous over single-file for gigantic datasets (100s of GB)
2. One file per column allows an administrator to control permissions for each column.
3. Having one file per column allows extending the dataset with new data more easily.

In short, NFF has several purely theoretical advantages, whereas Jay has many practical ones.

## Jay format
```
  "Jay1\0\0\0\0"       -- 8 bytes
  [data section]       -- ...
  [meta section]       -- <meta size>
  <meta size>          -- 8 bytes
  "\0\0\0\0Jay1"       -- 8 bytes
```
* The size of the file must be a multiple of 8 bytes, and all sections are aligned at 8-byte boundaries.
* The file begins and ends with `"Jay1"` string padded with 4 NUL bytes (`\0`). 
* Eight bytes before the last contain the size of the meta section (as an `int64` written in little-endian format). 
* The "meta section" is at the offset `16 + meta_size` from the end of the file, and it contains all meta information about the frame (including all column descriptors) serialized in Cap'n Proto format. 
* The portion of the file starting at offset 8 and ending at the beginning of the meta section is considered the data section. This section contains data buffers for each column. The exact location of each data buffer within the data section is stored in the meta section. The data buffers need not be adjacent to each other and are not necessarily stored in the same order as the columns themselves. However, each data buffer must be aligned at the 8-byte boundary.

## Saving a frame into a Jay file
* Create a "meta info" Cap'n Proto object
* For each column in the frame, write its buffers into the file, and its meta information into the meta object (including the locations of each buffer).
* Once all columns are written, write the meta object into the file
* Write the size of the meta, and the final signature.

## Opening a Jay file
* Open the file and verify its "Jay1" signatures at the beginning/end
* Read the meta size at the end of the file.
* Read the meta object with the information about the frame and all columns.
* At this point there are 2 choices: we can either memmap the entire file, and create each Column object as a subview into the large memmapped region, or memmap each portion of the file into a separate lazy column (similar how we do with NFF).

## Additional remarks
* This proposal does not yet specify the exact structure of the meta information, but generally it will follow the internal representation of the `DataTable` object.
* #1054 is a pre-requisite.
* #958 seeks to change the internal representation of string columns, and is better to do together with the switch to Jay, so as to avoid supporting too many different formats at the same time.
* New functionality will be exposed via standard `dt.open()` / `Frame.save()`. Ability to open NFF format will still be available for some time with `dt.open_nff()`.
* Issue #112 will become irrelevant.

[https://github.com/hazelcast/hazelcast-python-client/blob/master/hazelcast/cluster.py#L103](https://github.com/hazelcast/hazelcast-python-client/blob/master/hazelcast/cluster.py#L103)

I think there is a bug here, current_attempt should be increased after trying all addresses, not after each one. Also according to the java client possible addresses should be called each time it fails to connect to cluster after trying all possible addresses. So it should be called attemp_limit times I guess.

issue_title: Excessively long execution time for galsim.Kolmogorov

```
Python 3.6.2 |Continuum Analytics, Inc.| (default, Jul 20 2017, 13:51:32)

issue_title: Principal direction extents for a neuron as a whole
issue_body:
It would be useful to be able to calculate the principal direction extents for a whole neuron.

Right now the neurites are treated separately, calculating the principal components and the projection of points upon the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix of the points that constitute the neurite. 

However, sometimes one would like to calculate the extent of a neuron as a whole.

Thus, the feature could calculate the pcds for a neuron if a neuron is passed and for separete neurites if a set of neurites is passed.

* Data Sets
* Access Groups

These include traditional GIS operations and common database operations like JOINs. [Geopandas](http://geopandas.org/geometric_manipulations.html) has some nice functionality for operations like this, as does pandas. Here we want to take advantage of CARTO's cloud-based database to perform these methods, while being careful to stay in a Pythonic syntax like you see in pandas.

## PostGIS

- [ ] Spatial JOIN
- [ ] Intersects (give back all geometries that intersect geometries in a separate table -- or not)
- [ ] Nearest (give back the n-nearest geometries to another geometry)
- [ ] Non-spatial JOIN
- [ ] Envelope / Extent / Bounding Box
- [ ] Convex Hull
- [ ] Union
- [ ] Difference

## PostgreSQL

- [ ] Attribute JOIN
- [ ] Aggregations / GROUP BY / Etc.

## Location-data Services

- [ ] Geocoding
- [ ] Routing
- [ ] Isochrones

See #253 for a prototype of these operations in action.

@michellemho, could you help add to this list of popular and feasible operations that we could do in cartoframes?

cc @stuartlynn

For personal reasons, I wanted to call the `PandasCSVRenderer` on a dataframe outside of a view.

Because `renderer_context` is defaulted to `None`, the following code will throw `TypeError: argument of type 'NoneType' is not iterable`:

```python
from rest_pandas import renderers


data_frame = pandas.DataFrame(some_data)
print renderers.PandasCSVRenderer().render(data_frame)
>>>TypeError: argument of type 'NoneType' is not iterable
```

I suggest two options to fix the following code:
https://github.com/wq/django-rest-pandas/blob/edd3225acb21fcb0e36d8889d7bdfa5dedc5d67b/rest_pandas/renderers.py#L30

1. Change the if statement:
```python
if renderer_context and 'response' in renderer_context:
```
2. Add the following at the top of the function:
```python
if renderer_context is None:
    renreder_context = {}
...
```

Currently DyNet supports broadcasting for all operations across mini-batches, but only supports broadcasting within tensors for the `colwise_sum` operation and the bias term for `affine_transform`. issue_title: Behavior of s-unit setter/getter in state-less RNNBuilders
issue_body:
In some *state-less* `RNNBuilder` implementations (e.g. `SimpleRNNBuilder` or `GRUBuilder`) also have `get_s/set_s_impl` implementations, and they are basically coded as the redirect to `get_h/set_h_impl`. This (especially the setter) brings a side-effect against *h*-units, and maybe it sometimes could be an undesirable behavior.

I guess it is better that the `get_s` returns 0 and `set_s_impl` behaves no action if the RNN cell does not have *s*-units. How do maintainers think?

issue_title: Pub/Sub: subscriber waiting on future.result() blocks forever
issue_body:
when pubsub endpoints changed recently, it broke our code (after working fine for about a year).  we have ported our code to the new async API in 0.29.2 to get it working again.

following the documentation, we're calling `.open()` to register a callback; when run, it stores the message locally, then calls `message.ack()`.  in the main loop, we're waiting on the Future's `.result()` to be returned so we can do some bottom-half processing on the stored message.  however, our subscriber blocks forever waiting for the Future's .result() method to return, and I don't think this is how it's supposed to work based on the documentation:

> The `pubsub_v1.subscriber.policy.thread.Policy.open` method returns a `pubsub_v1.subscriber.futures.Future`, which is both the interface to wait on messages (e.g. block the primary thread) and to address exceptions.
> 
> To block the thread you are in while messages are coming in the stream, use the `.pubsub_v1.subscriber.futures.Future.result` method [...] You can also use this for error handling; any exceptions that crop up on a thread will be set on the future.

if it's "the interface to wait on messages," we're quite puzzled, because it never returns any results; it seems to block forever.  note that the same call in the publisher *does not* block; it returns the published message ID right away, which is what we expect.  only the subscriber has this strange behavior of never returning the result.

distilled to simple test case, here's the publisher:
```
topic_path = 'projects/ourproject/topics/test12345'
publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
publisher.publish(topic_path, b'New Message {0}'.format(datetime.now()))
```

and the subscriber (run this first to create the topic):
```
def callback(message):
    print 'Callback triggered'
    print message
    message.ack()

topic_path = 'projects/ourproject/topics/test12345'
subscription_path = 'projects/ourproject/subscriptions/test12345'

publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
publisher.create_topic(topic_path)

subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
subscriber.create_subscription(subscription_path, topic_path)
subscription = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path)

future = subscription.open(callback)
result = future.result()
```
^^^ this is where it hangs forever, although it continues to print messages when the callback runs, on each receive.  originally we thought it was because we're using a ThreadPool and calling these via `.map()`, it might be deadlocked somehow, but after reduction to a toy demo with single thread, single subscriber, no other work being done, it's still blocking forever.  here's the google lib versions:

```
 $ pip freeze | grep google
google-api-core==0.1.2
google-auth==1.2.1
google-auth-oauthlib==0.2.0
google-cloud-core==0.28.0
google-cloud-pubsub==0.29.2
google-compute-engine==2.7.1
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.3
grpc-google-iam-v1==0.11.4
```

are we using the interface incorrectly? is `future.result()` supposed to block forever? if we're supposed to do *all* the work in the subscriber callback, and just use `result` to catch exceptions later, then how do we actually exit the blocking call when all the work is done? we have noticed that doing our own `.set_result()` in the handler does cause `.result()` to return, are we supposed to be setting the result ourselves like this?

not sure if this is user error, or a bug.  I see the patch in #3888 that seems to be the one adding this feature of returning the Future, and the following quote:

> I do think it is fine to have a future that never has a result (or only gets one if you explicitly close)

so I'm guessing that we're using it wrong and supposed to only use this for exceptions.  If so, suggest some language might be added to the docs, that the Future should *not* be used to wait on messages themselves, but actually to wait on closure of the subscription.

issue_title: BigTable: On read_row(), provide default to retrieve only most recent cell values
issue_labels: [{'id': 210703442, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigtable', 'name': 'api: bigtable', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 461002860, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/performance', 'name': 'performance', 'color': 'bfdadc', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20feature%20request', 'name': 'type: feature request', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The current signature on the Bigtable `Table` [`read_row()` method][2] has a default of `filter_=None`:

```python
def read_row(self, row_key, filter_=None):
    ...
```

In cases where a cell value may have been updated multiple times, the default will be to return the full time series with timestamps for each value which can slow down read performance in a non-obvious way.  

In the current Python API the [`cells()` method][1] on `row_data` (`PartialRowData`) makes a deep copy of the cells, which compounds the performance issue.  

```python
@property
def cells(self):
    """Property returning all the cells accumulated on this partial row.

    :rtype: dict
    :returns: Dictionary of the :class:`Cell` objects accumulated. This
              dictionary has two-levels of keys (first for column families
              and second for column names/qualifiers within a family). For
              a given column, a list of :class:`Cell` objects is stored.
    """
    return copy.deepcopy(self._cells)
```

Consider:

1. Making a default filter on `read_row()` to retrieve only the most recent value of any cell unless the full or partial time series is requested.
1. Allowing a `ColumnFamily` to implicitly or explicitly limit cells to only one value (no timeseries).
1. Adding a `cell_value(column_family_id, column, index=0)` method to `row_data` (`PartialRowData`) to allow more efficient retrieval of a single cell value.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/bigtable-0.28.1/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/row_data.py#L153-L163
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/bigtable-0.28.1/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/table.py#L246
